reasoning:
The threshold for all resolution dates is fixed to the 30-day average of fatalities in the 360 days prior to March 2, 2025. This reference period includes the mass Gen Z anti-tax protests in June/July 2024, which resulted in scores of fatalities and artificially pushed the mean daily fatality rate upward (to roughly 61.9 per 30 days). 

Consequently, the distribution of 30-day fatalities is right-skewed. Evidence from ACLED reports outside of the protest period indicates that "normal" 30-day periods typically experience between 50 and 68 fatalities (with an estimated median around 55-58), mostly due to al-Shabaab raids in the east, pastoralist militia banditry in the Rift Valley, and local police clashes. Because the mean is higher than the median, a randomly selected 30-day period has a less than 50% chance of exceeding the 61.9 threshold. I estimate the base rate of exceeding this threshold in a normal month at roughly 38%.

For the imminent March 9 deadline (covering Feb 7 - March 8), 23 of the 30 days have already passed as of the forecast date. Based on targeted news searches, there have been no major spikes in mass casualty events (e.g., large-scale protests or major terror attacks) in February 2025. Without a significant incident to push counts above the baseline 1.6 - 1.8 deaths/day, this period is highly likely to fall short of 62 fatalities. Probability is thus lowered to 28%. 

For dates further in the future, uncertainty naturally increases and regresses towards the 0.50 mark as we cannot foresee short-term spikes in violence. Therefore, probabilities scale up gradually from 0.36 to 0.46 for subsequent dates.
